channel.title: Ajahn Funn Ācāro Quotes
channel.description: Dhamma quotes by Ajahn Funn Ācāro, updated every 24 hours

item.title: Ajahn Funn: This is one who has reached the Buddha, Dhamma, and Saṅgha: osaṭhaṃ uttamaṃ varaṃ — the supreme, excellent medicine. Cittaṃ deva-manussānaṃ — the…
item.description:
Buddho, Buddho: Maintaining Knowing Continuously

This is one who has reached the Buddha, Dhamma, and Saṅgha: osaṭhaṃ uttamaṃ varaṃ — the supreme, excellent medicine. Cittaṃ deva-manussānaṃ — the mind of devas and humans. Sabbe dukkhā, sabbe bhayā, sabbe rogā, bhavantu te vinassantu — may all sufferings, all fears, all diseases be destroyed and perish. All that happens because of a peaceful mind. The peaceful mind calms, quenches, and extinguishes all these worthless defilements.

Meditate on buddho, buddho — the heart becomes joyful. Buddho — the Knower. Know it completely. Don’t hide it. When suffering arises, know it. When pleasure arises, know it. When goodness arises, know it. Buddho, buddho — however it is, know it all. Wherever there is a problem, know it; wherever there is no problem, know it. Maintain that knowing continuously — that is called buddho. See that knowing existent, present. If it’s neutral, just know it. Direct your attention inward. If it’s dark, know it. If it’s bright, know it. The Knower — buddho — knows that. When it’s bright, know that it’s bright. Don’t take brightness as self.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Their minds were solid and still, in a state of virtue and concentration. They had abandoned the preoccupations of perception, of defilement, craving,…
item.description:
What Merit Is Like: A Heart That Is Happy and at Ease

Their minds were solid and still, in a state of virtue and concentration. They had abandoned the preoccupations of perception, of defilement, craving, passion, greed, aversion, delusion, ignorance, clinging, becoming, and birth. That’s how they reached the paths and their fruitions. Their minds were at peace—after the Buddha had taught them nothing more than that.

So try to understand these things, these things that we always have with us. Examine your body as it is right now. Examine your mind. What level of becoming are you living in right now? The level of sensuality? The level of form? The level of formlessness? Try to understand where your mind is dwelling. You’ve made merit, so is your mind meritorious yet? Or not? Look into the matter.

What is a meritorious mind like? It’s a good mind, a mind with a sense of well-being, of being at ease. Peaceful and undisturbed, untroubled, Buddho, a blossoming mind at ease, free from difficulties, free from irritation: That’s what merit is. So many people do merit but then complain that they can’t see what kind of thing merit is. But how can it be a thing, merit? People want to know what kind of thing merit is. If it’s a thing, it’s the human body—every human body sitting here, with your black hair and bent necks. Each human body sitting here is a body of merit: You’ve done good, you feel happy, you feel at ease. Your heart and mind are blooming. This is what Buddho is like—free from difficulties, free from irritation.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Why do we look closely? In order to abandon clinging to the aggregates (upādānakkhandha), to abandon personality view (sakkāyadiṭṭhi), to abandon skeptical doubt…
item.description:
Abandoning Personality View by Seeing the Truth of Hair, Nails, Teeth, Skin

Why do we look closely? In order to abandon clinging to the aggregates (upādānakkhandha), to abandon personality view (sakkāyadiṭṭhi), to abandon skeptical doubt (vicikicchā), to abandon attachment to rites and rituals (sīlabbataparāmāsa) – not anything far away. We bring it in, we contemplate the primary meditation subject (mūla-kammaṭṭhāna). When we ordained, the teacher taught this mūla-kammaṭṭhāna. We think that a kammaṭṭhāna monk is one who lives in the forest, but no – everything is kammaṭṭhāna. The mūla-kammaṭṭhāna teaches: kesā – hair. Whose hair? Where is it? Wherever the hair is, right there is the kammaṭṭhāna. Lomā – body hair – the kammaṭṭhāna of body hair. Nakhā – nails – the kammaṭṭhāna of nails. Where is it? Dantā – teeth – the kammaṭṭhāna of teeth. Where is it? Taco – skin – the kammaṭṭhāna of skin. Where is it? It envelops our whole body. These five things – this is the kammaṭṭhāna. Why did the Buddha teach the mūla-kammaṭṭhāna? Because these are the root cause that deludes beings in this world. Seeing hair, body hair, nails, teeth, skin, they mistake them for a self, for a being, for a person – “me” and “him,” “beautiful” and “handsome.” When we see them like that, we become deluded.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: When we train ourselves in kāyaviveka and cittaviveka, developing and cultivating them diligently with non-negligence (appamāda) in the practice of bodily and mental…
item.description:
Beings Are Born Through the Objects to Which They Are Attached

When we train ourselves in kāyaviveka and cittaviveka, developing and cultivating them diligently with non-negligence (appamāda) in the practice of bodily and mental seclusion, then we will attain upadhiviveka (seclusion from the substrata of existence), which is the supreme peace. We should practice in this way, on the majjhimāpaṭipadā — i.e., practice the middle way. Then our mind will not be deluded and attached to the kāmaloka (realm of sense-desire), rūpaloka (realm of form), or arūpaloka (realm of formlessness) — the three realms of existence (bhava) where beings wander, still deluded and attached to sense-objects (kāmārammaṇa), form-objects (rūpārammaṇa), and formless-objects (arūpārammaṇa). Among the beings that wander and take birth in the kāmaloka, there are forms, sounds, smells, tastes, and tactile objects — the sense-objects that serve as their dwelling. Beings who are negligent, intoxicated, delighting in and deludedly attached to any kind of object — they are born through that object. Their aging is through that object, their sickness is through that object, their death is through that object to which they are attached. As long as this defilement of delusion (moha) exists, birth and death, death and birth, cycling and revolving without end will not cease.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Who is happy right now? Who is good, who is not good – look. It is not because of possessions or money that…
item.description:
Happiness Is Not Because of Possessions – It Is the Mind Peaceful

Who is happy right now? Who is good, who is not good – look. It is not because of possessions or money that one is good. It is not because of possessions or money that one is happy. It is not because of possessions or money that one suffers. Look. Are buildings and restaurants suffering? No, they're not. It's the mind.

That's it – suffering is the mind. Happiness is the mind. Why happy? Happy because the mind is peaceful. Why suffering? Suffering because the mind is not peaceful. It goes on creating kamma and enmity endlessly. Old kamma never ends, and new kamma keeps being added. Will it ever end? Stop it now! Kamma – don't cling to kamma. Whatever you cling to, that becomes kamma. Let it all go. Release it all. Abandon it all. Leave only Buddho, the single knower.

Alright, I will explain no further. Each of you listen to your own mind. Make it firm. Make it confident. Gain your own footing and foundation.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: The sacred things of the whole world exist already: the Buddha, the Dhamma, the Saṅgha. These are sacred and powerful. In the three…
item.description:
The Buddha Is the Excellent Refuge: No Other Refuge in the Three Worlds

The sacred things of the whole world exist already: the Buddha, the Dhamma, the Saṅgha. These are sacred and powerful. In the three worlds, the Buddha is supreme over all. Sabbabuddhānubhāvena – by the power of the Buddha. Sabbadhammānubhāvena – by the power of the Dhamma. Sabbasaṅghānubhāvena – by the power of the Noble Saṅgha. Consider this, all of you. Be people who understand. Buddho me nātho, Dhammo me nātho. Nātha means the great refuge. I see no other thing with greater power. Natthi me saraṇaṃ aññaṃ – I have no other refuge. In the three worlds, there is nothing else to rely on. Only the Buddha is the excellent refuge. Buddho me saraṇaṃ varaṃ – only the Buddha, Dhamma, and Saṅgha are the excellent refuge. Now that you have all listened to this exhortation and instruction – the Dhamma, the teaching of the Buddha, which has been explained and demonstrated to this extent – it is enough as a basis for practice. Having understood the essential points of the Buddha’s teaching, you see that this body and mind are the foundation of the religion, the foundation of the Path and its Fruition. Having heard this, apply wise attention (yoniso manasikāra), determine and remember it, then take it into practice, training yourselves in accordance with the Dhamma teaching of the Supreme Perfectly Enlightened Buddha, to achieve the ultimate result, which is the quality of non-negligence (appamāda dhamma). When you all possess non-negligence, from now on you will meet only with happiness and prosperity. And so it is.

Cattāro dhammā vaḍḍhanti – āyu, vaṇṇo, sukhaṃ, balaṃ.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Today people regard it as New Year's Day, so they close up work, stop working, and go out touring. In truth, originally the…
item.description:
New Year's Day: A Time for Merit, Not Amusement

Today people regard it as New Year's Day, so they close up work, stop working, and go out touring. In truth, originally the closing of work was so that people could make merit, perform wholesome deeds, and cultivate virtue and goodness. Nowadays, people treat it as a time for amusement. They close up work and then go out to play. Each person will play, travel, gamble, or do whatever else – only seeking ruin for themselves. Therefore, our country has become chaotic and distressed. Really, it's a holiday so that we can approach virtue and goodness, go to monasteries, make merit, perform wholesome deeds, observe the precepts, and rest from the work we've been doing all year. We observe a day or two – for what purpose? We come to the monastery to measure and see: the work we've done for nearly a year, as long as this – some of it good, some not good – we come to know how it is. We come to the monastery to look at all things. What will we take along? We take what follows us. Measure and see.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: And as for “good destiny” or “bad destiny” – people lie and deceive each other causing trouble and suffering. In Buddhism, look at…
item.description:
Good Destiny and Bad Destiny Are in the Mind, Not in the Sky

And as for “good destiny” or “bad destiny” – people lie and deceive each other causing trouble and suffering. In Buddhism, look at destiny. It does not come from the sky or air. Look: what is good destiny? In short, good destiny means our mind is good, we have happiness and ease. When our mind is happy and at ease, then whatever we do is easy, work is easy, the country is at ease. That is called good destiny. Bad destiny means our mind is not good, our mind is suffering, troubled, agitated – that is bad destiny. Whatever we do is not good, whatever we seek is not good. Look right here. Why ask anyone else to look? Everyone sitting here – both monks, novices, laymen, laywomen – your good destiny is right here. Do not contemplate anything else. And do not join the sects of the titthiyas or nigaṇṭhas, those outside the religion. If you go and hold to that, you become cut off from Buddhism. This is how one who practices the religion must hold.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: Focus your contemplation so that you know and see. When we analyze and separate those things, our mind becomes one. There is no…
item.description:
When the Mind Is Completely Empty, There Is Nothing – No Danger, No Enmity

Focus your contemplation so that you know and see. When we analyze and separate those things, our mind becomes one. There is no person, no self, no being, no owner, no person, no home, no city – not a single thing. The mind lets go of everything. When the mind is completely empty, there is nothing – no danger, no enmity.

Now, we go and create kamma, create enmity, assuming this and that. It's because we are deluded by conventions. Beings are stuck here, sunk in this ocean. They cannot cross. We must transcend conventions. Ask yourself: Are you a mountain? Are you a kham bush? Doesn't it say "no"? Or what? Ask it: Are you a kham bush? Silence. See? It doesn't say anything. We go and label it, but it remains neutral. It doesn't say it's a kham bush. The kham tree doesn't say either. Ask: Are you earth? See? It doesn't say anything at all. Are you water? Are you wind? Are you fire? It doesn't say anything. We simply get deluded on our own. That's why we must analyze and separate so as not to be deluded.

item.title: Ajahn Funn: This body is also concocted into form and name (nāma-rūpa). In time, it ceases. Where does it go? Earth returns to earth, water…
item.description:
Vipassanā: Knowing the Arising and Ceasing of Saṅkhārā

This body is also concocted into form and name (nāma-rūpa). In time, it ceases. Where does it go? Earth returns to earth, water to water, wind to wind, fire to fire, the space element to space. It all returns. That’s how it is.

Therefore, we are taught to know the aggregate of conditioned phenomena thoroughly. This is the person of wisdom who fully knows the aggregate of conditioned phenomena. Whatever arises, know it completely. Know the conditioned phenomena thoroughly. Know consciousness (viññāṇa) thoroughly — the one that relinks, the one that compounds. These — perception (saññā), mental formations (saṅkhārā), consciousness — know them all. See that they are all impermanent. Whatever is impermanent, that is suffering. Whatever is suffering, that is not self, not a being. So contemplate so that it knows like that. Don’t let it arise. If it does arise, know it in time. That is called vipassanāñāṇa — the knowledge of insight. Insight (vipassanā) for what purpose? To know the arising and ceasing of saṅkhārā, to know the suffering and danger in saṅkhārā, to know the peril in saṅkhārā. This is the arising of insight knowledge: knowledge of arising and ceasing in saṅkhārā. Wherever it arises, we cease it right there. Arise and cease simultaneously.
